coredata.dc:title: A shining proposal for the detection of dissolved O2 in aqueous medium: Self-calibrated optical sensing via a covalent hybrid structure of carbon-dots&Ru
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yanping
Liu, Xuelian
Jiang, Yuanyuan
Mao, Li
Wang, Hongjun
Liu, Liang
coredata.dc:description: O2 is a life-supporting gas and has been widely recognized as an important analyte in life science, medical care and environmental science. Optical sensing for gaseous oxygen has been widely reported owing to the simple, cost-effective and easy-to-go procedure. On the other hand, optical sensors for dissolved oxygen in aqueous media have been rarely reported, since most of them are incompatible with water, leading to poor sensitivity and linearity. In this effort, we tried the combination of Ru(II)-bpy complex and carbon dots (CDs) via covalent bonds, where bpy = bipyridine. A hybrid structure, named as Ru@CD, was constructed for the detection of dissolved oxygen, using Ru(II)-bpy as sensing probe and CDs as water-compatible supporting matrix. Ru@CD was carefully characterized to confirm its hybrid structure. Detailed analysis suggested that its emission showed self-calibrated sensing signals for dissolved oxygen. A good linearity of 99.1% was realized. Its sensitivity (3.18) was higher than most literature values for dissolved oxygen detection. Its working equation was confirmed as a corrected Stern-Volmer equation (Lehrer mode). Good selectivity and signal stability were observed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dissolved oxygen
Optical sensing
Composite materials
CDs and Ru(II)

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Emission spectra, quantum yield, lifetime and FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) efficiency
Sensing performance of Ru@CD for dissolved oxygen
Stern-Volmer fitting with ideal equation and corrected equation
Practical sensing in running water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of CDs (up three photos) and Ru@CD (down three photos), including size distribution.
XRD patterns of CDs and Ru@CD.
IR spectra of CDs, Ru(II) probe, and Ru@CD.
Emission and absorption spectra of CDs and Ru(II) probe.
Emission spectra of Ru@CD in aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) with excitation wavelength of (350 nm). Inset: photos of Ru@CD bubbled by pure N2 (left) and pure O2 (right).
Emission decay dynamics of CDs and Ru@CD.

Self-calibreated emission spectra of Ru@CD (1 mg/mL) under various dissolved oxygen concentrations. For original ones, check Figure S1 (Supporting Information). Inset: F627/F432 against CO2 variation.
R0/R against CO2, fitted with ideal Stern-Volmer equation (Formula (4)).
R0/R against CO2, fitted with corrected Stern-Volmer equation (Formula (6)).
R0/R against CO2, fitted with corrected Stern-Volmer equation (Formula (7)).
F627/F432 contrast bars of Ru@CD (0.1 mg/mL) under interferents (100 μmol/L).
F627/F432 values of Ru@CD aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL) when being bubbled by pure N2 and pure O2 streams periodically.
Working strategy of Ru@CD oxygen sensing.
Emission decay dynamics of CDs and Ru@CD.
Fitting parameters with ideal Stern-Volmer equation (Formula (4)) and corrected Stern-Volmer equations (Formulas (6) and (7)).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a, ideal Stern-Volmer equation has only one kind of sensing site, so KSV2 = 0.
b, in corrected Stern-Volmer equation (Formula (7)), it is assumed that sensing site 2 is insensitive towards oxygen, so KSV2 = 0.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dissolved oxygen concentrations determined by Winkler method and Ru@CD.
Sensing performance comparison of Ru@CD and literature systems.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 1, this work; 2, Ref. [19]; 3, Ref. [20]; 4, Ref. [21]; 5, Ref. [22]; 6, Ref. [23]; 7, Ref. [24]; 8, Ref. [25]; 9, Ref. [26]; 10, Ref. [27]; 11, Ref. [13], here the quenching-based sensitivity is 18.0 and the self-calibrated sensitivity is 1.0.

head.ce:title: A shining proposal for the detection of dissolved O2 in aqueous medium: Self-calibrated optical sensing via a covalent hybrid structure of carbon-dots&Ru

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Tide Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., No. 8 Rongjing East Street, Beijing Economic Technological Development Area, Beijing 100176, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Tide Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., No. 8 Rongjing East Street, Beijing Economic Technological Development Area

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Tide Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., No. 8 Rongjing East Street, Beijing Economic Technological Development Area, Beijing 100176, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yanping Zhao and Xuelian Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: O2 is a life-supporting gas and has been widely recognized as an important analyte in life science, medical care and environmental science. Optical sensing for gaseous oxygen has been widely reported owing to the simple, cost-effective and easy-to-go procedure. On the other hand, optical sensors for dissolved oxygen in aqueous media have been rarely reported, since most of them are incompatible with water, leading to poor sensitivity and linearity. In this effort, we tried the combination of Ru(II)-bpy complex and carbon dots (CDs) via covalent bonds, where bpy = bipyridine. A hybrid structure, named as Ru@CD, was constructed for the detection of dissolved oxygen, using Ru(II)-bpy as sensing probe and CDs as water-compatible supporting matrix. Ru@CD was carefully characterized to confirm its hybrid structure. Detailed analysis suggested that its emission showed self-calibrated sensing signals for dissolved oxygen. A good linearity of 99.1% was realized. Its sensitivity (3.18) was higher than most literature values for dissolved oxygen detection. Its working equation was confirmed as a corrected Stern-Volmer equation (Lehrer mode). Good selectivity and signal stability were observed.

ce:section.ce:para: O2 gas has been known as an essential life-supporting gas for living animals, including animals, plants and even microbes on land. O2 gas can be dissolved in water with a concentration of ~ 9 mg/L. Such dissolved oxygen is equally important to animals in rivers, lakes and seas to support their normal growth and living. For example, fishes generally need dissolved oxygen higher than 4 mg/L to support their normal biological activity. If dissolved oxygen concentration is lower than 2 mg/L, their activity will be limited, and an even lower dissolved oxygen concentration can lead to their suffocation or death [1]. This fact makes dissolved oxygen an important indicator for water environment. In addition, a low level of local dissolved oxygen concentration has been reported as a sign for tumors in human body [2]. The sensitive and selective determination for dissolved oxygen is thus an important issue in life science, medical care and environmental science.

There have been traditional analytical methods for dissolved oxygen, such as iodimetric method, electrochemical method and so on [3,4]. Although iodimetric method gives precise result, it costs time and sample, which makes it inappropriate for on-line detection. As for electrochemical method, such as Clark amperometric electrode, its precision strongly depends on the surface status of electrodes. Given these limitations, optical sensing has been proposed as an attractive method for oxygen determination due to its virtues of fast, effective and non-invasive detection [5]. Its sensing procedure is generally described as a dynamic collision between excited probe and ground state O2 (3O2), where excited probe is attacked by ground state O2 and loses its energy, showing probe emission quenching effect. Since a long excited state lifetime offers a high collision probability and consequently a high sensitivity, oxygen sensing probes are generally phosphorescent ones [6]. Luminescent Ru(II)-bpy (bpy = bipyridine) complexes have been reported as promising probes owing to their long-lived phosphorescent emission (~10 μs) based on metal-to-ligand-charge-transfer (MLCT) [6,7]. A typical sensing procedure has been proposed as Ru(II)* + 3O2 → Ru(II) + 1O2*, where “*” means excited state.

To ensure fast and fluent O2 diffusion, Ru(II)-bpy probes are generally embedded into supporting matrix to form a hybrid structure, hoping to combine features of each component at nanometer or molecular level. In this case, various optical oxygen sensing structures have been developed and reported [8,9,10]. Many literatures focusing on the development of dissolved oxygen have been reported based on polymers, micelles and nanoparticles. For example Vinogradov and coworkers reported their phosphorescent Pd-probes for biological oximetry and tumor imaging [11]. Wang and coworkers reported two-photon oxygen nanosensors based on a conjugated fluorescent polymer doped with platinum porphyrins [12]. The ratiometric extracellular oxygen sensing has been reported by Tian and coworkers using poly(ε-caprolactone)-containing graft copolymers [13]. Wolfbeis and coworkers have summarized recent progress of optical methods for sensing and imaging oxygen, including materials, spectroscopies and applications [14]. Never the less, there are still problems to be solved. First, the linearity of working curve is yet to be satisfied. Second, most sensing systems have only one dominant emission band for sensing quantification. Considering that probe emission is affected by various factors such as equipment condition, sample concentration and status, these optical sensing systems generally need auxiliary reference before giving previse quantification results.

To solve the above two problems, an ideal optical sensing system for dissolved oxygen should be composed of a stable and long-lived probe, a reference emission and a water-compatible supporting matrix. In this effort, we intend to try the combination of Ru(II)-bpy complex and carbon dots (CDs) via covalent bonds. CDs have recently been nominated as an attractive material owing to their strong and stable emission, good compatibility with water [15,16]. Upon the covalent bonding between CDs and Ru(II)-bpy, their phase separation can be avoided. The good water compatibility of CDs and Ru(II)-bpy makes sure an effective collision with analyte O2 molecules. The strong emission of CDs has a short excited state lifetime (~several nanoseconds), which is too soon for O2 attack, making CDs emission immune to O2 existence. In other words, CDs emission can be used as an inner reference for Ru(II)-bpy optical sensing. In this case, a hybrid structure will be constructed for the detection of dissolved oxygen, using Ru(II)-bpy as sensing probe and CDs as supporting matrix.
Scheme 1 shows a detailed synthetic procedure for this hybrid structure, named as Ru@CD. A brief description about Ru@CD synthesis and sensing operation is listed below.

1,10-phenanthrolin-5-amine (Phen-NH2) was synthesized following a two-step procedure. 1,10-phenanthroline (5 g) was cooled by ice bath. Concentrated H2SO4 (8 mL) was dropwise added, then fuming nitric acid (30 mL) was dropwise added under stirring. This mixture was stirred under ice bath for 30 min and then at 90 °C for 30 h. The resulting mixture was poured into ice water (100 mL) and then nitrilized with NaOH solution. Solid product was collected, washed with water and recrystallized with ethanol to give 5-nitro-1,10-phenanthroline as yellow powder. Yield = 63%. 1H NMR(CDCl3), δ:7.76 (m, 2H), 8.76 (d, 1H), 8.77 (d, 1H), 9.03 (s, 1H), 9.24 (d, 1H), 9.28 (d, 1H).

The above obtained 5-nitro-1,10-phenanthroline (1.5 g) was dissolved in hot ethanol (20 mL). 5% Pd/C (0.3 g), hydrazine hydrate (50%, 15 mL) and ethanol (10 mL) were mixed together and heated to 70 °C. These two solutions were mixed together and heated at 90 °C for 10 h. The resulting solution was filtered to remove Pd/C. Yellow powder Phen-NH2 was obtained after extracting solvent under reduced pressure. Yield = 78%. 1H NMR(CDCl3), δ:6.14 (s, 2H), 6.86 (s, 1H), 7.53 (m, 1H), 7.74 (m, 1H), 8.06 (d, 1H), 8.65 (m, 2H) , 9.04 (d, 1H).

4-((1,10-phenanthrolin-5-yl)amino)-4-oxobutanoic acid (Phen-COOH) was synthesized following below method. Butanedioic anhydride (2.56 g) was dissolved in a mixture of acetonitrile/dimethyl formamidine (10:1, 15 mL). Then Phen-NH2 (1 g) was added. This mixture was heated to reflux and kept for 3 h. This mixture was placed at 0 °C overnight. Solid product was collected, washed with acetonitrile and water to give Phen-COOH. Yield = 67%. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d6 ) δ (ppm): 2.64 (t, 2H), 2.81 (t, 2H), 7.74 (dd, 1H), 7.81 (dd, 1H), 8.16 (s, 1H), 8.45 (dd, 1H), 8.67 (dd, 1H), 9.04 (dd, 1H), 9.13 (dd, 1H), 10.21 (s,1H), 12.21 (s, 1H).
Ru(bpy)2Cl2⋅2H2O was synthesized following a literature procedure with RuCl3⋅6H2O and 2,2′-bipyridine as starting compounds [5]. Yield = 45%.

CDs were prepared as follows [16]. 0.84 g of citric acid and 1.02 g of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of double-distilled water, then the solution was decanted into a Teflon-lined autoclave (30 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally, the dispersion was dialyzed [MWCO (molecular weight cut-off) = 1000] against 500 mL of pure water for three times. Product was freeze dried to obtain brown powder (Yield = 0.16 g) which was then dispersed in water again in a concentration of 10 mg/mL for further use.

A mixture of Ru(bpy)2Cl2⋅2H2O (0.26 g), Phen-COOH (0.15 g) and ethanol (20 mL) was heated at 80 °C for 12 h under N2 atmosphere until the solution became a red transparent one. This solution was filtered and then vaporized. Solid product was washed with hexane and dried. Double-distilled water (10 mL) was added. The resulting solution was placed in a centrifuge (5000 × g) for 15 min. The upper transparent solution was collected and dried under reduced pressure to give the Ru(II) probe. Yield = 64%.

Ru(II) probe (0.3 g) was dissolved in double-distilled water (10 mL). Its pH was adjusted by dispersed HCl as ~ 6, then 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC, 0.75 g) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.45 g) was added. This mixture was stirred at room temperature for 45 min. Then CDs dispersion (10 mL, 10 mg/mL) was dropwise added and allowed to react at room temperature for 48 h. The resulting mixture was placed in a centrifuge (5500 × g) for 5 min. The upper transparent solution was dialyzed (MWCO = 1000) against 500 mL of pure water until no fluorescence was detected for the dispersion. The resulting solution was freeze dried (Yield = 0.15 g) and re-dispersed in boiled double-distilled water (10 mg/mL) for further use.

Double-distilled water was boiled and bubbled with pure N2 stream for 30 min to eliminate potential dissolved oxygen. The final concentration of dissolved oxygen was adjusted by bubbling dispersion with N2 and O2 streams. Each sample was equally divided and sealed in two Winkler bottles, one was determined by a standard analytical method for dissolved oxygen (GB7489-87, China), the other was mixed with Ru@CD dispersion. Ru@CD dispersion was bubbled with pure N2 stream for 30 min prior to use.

Luminescence quantum yield (Φ) was determined with quinine sulfate in 1.0 M sulfuric acid (Φr = 0.546) as reference standard. Sample and standard solutions were degassed with no less than four cycles. Quantum yield was determined according to
Φ s
=
Φ r
(
B r
/
B s
)
(
n s
/
n r
)
2
D s
/
D r

Here the subscripts s and r refer to sample and reference standard solution respectively, n is refractive index of the solvent, D is the integrated intensity, and Φ is luminescence quantum yield. The quantity B is calculated by B = 1 − 10□AL, where A is the absorption coefficient at the excitation wavelength and L is the optical length.

1H NMR spectra were provided by a Varian INOVA 300 spectrometer. XRD (X-ray diffraction) data were collected with a Bruker D4 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation, wavelength = 1.5405 Å, voltage = 40 kV, current = 30 mA. Fluorescence and excitation spectra were collected with Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer excited by a Xe lamp (150 W), excitation wavelength was set as 350 nm, slim was set as 5 nm, and voltage was set as 700 V. UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-3101 PC spectrophotometer. Elemental analysis was finished by a Carlo Erba 1106 elemtal analyzer. AFM (atomic force microscope) images were taken by a Bruker NanoScope V Multimode 8 microscope, using a probe of Bruker RTESP, f0 = 289 ~ 325 kHz, k = 20 ~ 80 N/m. Emission decay dynamics was performed by a FL920 spectrometer, using an excitation source of H lamp.

The oxygen sensing strategy of Ru@CD is depicted by Scheme 2 . The blue-emitting CDs are used as supporting matrix, its blue emission is supposed to be a constant one and thus used as an inner reference for self-calibrated sensing. While the red-emitting Ru(II) probe is responsible for oxygen sensing via a dynamic quenching collision with oxygen. These two components are covalently grafted together to avoid phase separation. It has been reported that the emissive performance of CDs depends on their size [16]. Aiming at a visual understanding on Ru@CD nanoparticles, their AFM (atomic force microscope) image, along with that of CDs, is shown as Fig. 1 . It is observed that CDs have a size distribution of 1–3 nm, which is consistent with literature case [16]. They are uniformly distributed on their supporting matrix, showing a homogeneous status. After the covalent grafting of Ru(II) probe, these carbon dots have preserved their morphology and size distribution well. A slight aggregation is observed for Ru@CD nanoparticles, owing to their surface modification which increases the interaction between Ru@CD nanoparticles. For a better understanding on Ru@CD nanoparticles, its XRD pattern is compared with that of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , both samples have shown a broad peak around 25 °(0.34 nm), which is consistent with literature report [16]. Such XRD pattern suggests that Ru@CD nanoparticles are mainly composed of amorphous carbon atoms, with rare well-resolved lattice fringers.

As depicted by Scheme 1, Ru(II) probe is covalently grafted onto CDs via a reaction between –COOH (from probe) and –NH2 (from CDs) bonds. To confirm this hypothesis, IR (infra-red) spectra of CDs, Ru(II) probe, and Ru@CD are compared in Fig. 3 . As for CDs, several characteristic bands have been observed, including stretching vibration of C-OH around 3400 cm−1, stretching vibration of C-H at 2930 cm−1, vibration of C = O around 1673 cm−1, bending vibration of N-H at 1600 cm−1 and asymmetric stretching vibration of C-NH-C at 1100 cm−1, respectively. These IR peaks are consistent with the microstructure of CDs [16]. As for Ru(II) probe, there are more characteristic bands, including stretching vibration of –OC-OH at 3335 cm−1, vibration of –CH2- at 2970 cm−1, stretching vibration of HO-C = O at 1768 cm−1, vibration of CO-NH at 1708 cm−1, vibration of C = O at 1649 cm−1 and bending vibration of N-H at 1590 cm−1, respectively. Most above mentioned IR peaks can be traced from the IR spectrum of Ru@CD, including 3428 cm−1 (-C-OH), 2970 cm−1 (–CH2-), 1632 cm−1 (C = O) and 1547 cm−1 (N-H). The characteristic IR peak for HO-C = O band around 1768 cm−1 is not found in the IR spectrum of Ru@CD. In addition, the IR intensity of N-H bending is obviously enhanced in Ru@CD, compared to that in CDs. It is thus confirmed that Ru(II) probe has been grafted with CDs via a covalent bond. Such covalent grafting can effectively avoid phase separation between these two components, and their composition can be fixed.

As for CDs, its elemental result is listed as C, 80.79%; H, 12.79%; N, 1.65%. After loading Ru(II) probe, the elemental result in Ru@CD is observed as C, 76.85%; H, 11.53%; N, 3.42%. Mathematically, Ru(II) probe loading level can be determined as ~ 15.6% in Ru@CD. This low value suggests that Ru(II) probe molecules are only grafted onto CDs surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission spectra, quantum yield, lifetime and FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
Emission and absorption spectra of CDs and Ru(II) probe are discussed firstly. It is observed from Fig. 4 that the Ru(II) probe has a broad absorption in UV region, extending to visible region of ~ 550 nm. It emission peaks at 592 nm, with emission quantum yield (QY) of 11%. Considering its large FWHM (full width at half maximum, 102 nm) and Stokes shift (42 nm), this emission is originated from MLCT (metal-to-ligand-charge-transfer) excited state [7]. CDs have two absorption maxima in UV (ultra-violet) region, peaking at 249 nm and 350 nm. Upon excitation wavelength of 350 nm, these CDs exhibit strong blue emission peaking at 442 nm, with QY of 72%. To evaluate the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency between a donor and an acceptor, energy transfer radius (R0) is usually mentioned, as described by Formula (1) and Formula (2). Here, Q0, J, κ2, nd, NA, λ, fd(λ), εA(λ) are donor intrinsic QY, spectral overlap integral between donor emission and acceptor absorption, mutual molecular orientation, solvent refraction index, Avogadro number, wavelength (nm), donor emission intensity and acceptor molar extinction efficient, respectively [17].
(1)
R 0 6
=
9
Q 0
k 2
J
( ln 10 )
128
π 5
n d 4
N A
(2)
J = ∫
f D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ 4
d λ

It is clear that J plays an important role in controlling FRET efficiency. CDs blue emission perfectly matches the absorption spectrum of Ru(II) probe, as shown in Fig. 4, indicating that there shall be an efficient FRET procedure from CDs to Ru(II) probe.

This hypothesis is firstly confirmed by the emission spectrum of Ru@CD shown in Fig. 5 . Upon excitation of 350 nm, Ru@CD shows two characteristic emission bands from its two components, CDs and Ru(II) probe. On the other hand, the emission from CDs component is blue shifted to 432 nm while that from Ru(II) component is red shifted to 627 nm. It is rational to assume that the electron-rich CDs donate electron-deficient Ru(II) probe, leading to such spectral shift. After being bubbled by pure N2 and pure O2 streams, Ru@CD suspension shows obvious spectral response. Considering that the emission of Ru@CD suspension samples may be affected by unstable excitation light or other systematical errors, Ru@CD emission intensity shall be corrected before evaluation. For a clear view on the emission intensity variation at 627 nm upon being bubbled by pure N2 and pure O2 streams, the emission intensity at 432 nm is normalized and used as a calibrating reference. With this constant CDs emission intensity, it is observed that the Ru(II) emission is quenched by O2 effectively, indicating its potential for oxygen sensing. Additionally, the intensity of CDs emission can be used as an ideal inner reference, so that self-calibrated oxygen sensing can be realized, which will be discussed in detail below.

For a further confirmation on the FRET procedure between CDs and Ru(II) probe, emission decay dynamics of CDs and Ru@CD are measured and shown in Fig. 6 . Their detailed fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 . CDs follow biexponential decay pattern with two decay centers, τ1 = 13.07 ns (weighting = 63.51%) and τ2 = 4.65 ns (weighting = 36.49%). Their average lifetime (τ) is calculated as 10.00 ns. These short decay procedures are too fast for O2 molecule collision, which explains the insensitivity of CDs emission towards O2. As for Ru@CD, interestingly, it follows monoexponential decay pattern, with lifetime of 5.03 ns. It is obvious that the long-lived decay center τ1 disappears in Ru@CD, which shall be attributed to the FRET procedure from CDs component to Ru(II) component. Corresponding energy transfer efficiency (η) is calculated by Formula (3). Here τ and τ’ mean the intrinsic lifetime and the lifetime with energy acceptor, respectively. This modest η value (49.7%) guarantees enough FRET from CDs to Ru(II) probe and strong CDs emission to construct self-calibrated signals.
(3)
η = 1 -
τ ′
/ τ

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of Ru@CD for dissolved oxygen

ce:section.ce:para: For a detailed evaluation on its sensing performance for dissolved oxygen, Ru@CD is dispersed in aqueous samples of various dissolved oxygen levels. Corresponding emission spectra are recorded. The original spectra can be found from Figure S1 (Supporting Information). It is observed that Ru(II) emission intensity at 627 nm is obviously quenched by increasing dissolved oxygen level. On the other hand, CDs emission intensity at 432 nm is varied as well owing to systematical error between each testing run (see Supporting Information for a detailed explanation). To eliminate such systematical error, CDs emission intensity at 432 nm is chosen as a calibrating reference, as above mentioned, corresponding normalized emission spectra are shown as Fig. 7 . In this self-calibrated view, CDs emission, including both band shape and intensity, remains nearly constant under increasing oxygen level. While, Ru(II) emission is decreased. This fact suggests that the Ru(II) emission is quenched by dissolved oxygen via a dynamic collision, which leads to the non-radiative decay of excited Ru(II) probe. On the other hand, such collision has slim effect on CDs emission or its FRET to Ru(II). The correlation between donor (CDs) and acceptor (Ru(II) probe) is well preserved. For the following discussion, self-calibrated value F627/F432 is used. A non-linear curve is observed for F627/F432 against dissolve oxygen concentration (CO2), as depicted by Fig. 7 inset. Within effective CO2 region of 0.72–42.14 mg/L, F627/F432 value varies between 3.18 and 0.70. With these values on hand, the sensing behavior of Ru@CD can be analyzed by Stern-Volmer equation below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stern-Volmer fitting with ideal equation and corrected equation
ce:section.ce:para:
The ideal Stern-Volmer equation (intensity form) is a linear curve with slope of KSV , as described as Formula (4). Here R is the detected F627/F432 value, KSV and CO2 are Stern-Volmer constant and dissolved oxygen concentration, respectively [6]. As for R0, it shall be the F627/F432 value with absolutely no dissolved oxygen.
(4)
R 0
/ R = 1 +
K
SV
∙
C
o 2

In this work, R0 is selected as the highest value (3.18) in effective CO2 region of 0.72–42.14 mg/L. R0/R against CO2 is plotted as Fig. 8 . Corresponding fitting parameters with Formula (4) are given in Table 2 . It is observed that these R0/R values match Formula (4) only within CO2 region no higher than 10 mg/L. Upon high oxygen concentrations, errors are obvious. Corresponding R2 is only 99.1%. In other words, this ideal Stern-Volmer equation should be corrected by considering more factors during oxygen sensing procedure.

The ideal Stern-Volmer equation is based on a hypothesis that probe molecules and quencher molecules are uniformly distributed, so that all probe molecules share the same quenching probability. If each probe molecule has its intrinsic quenching probability, the ideal Stern-Volmer equation shall be corrected as Formula (5). Here I0 is the emission intensity without quencher, I is detected emission intensity, CQ is quencher concentration, KSVi is the Stern-Volmer constant of each probe molecule, fi is ratio emission contribution of each probe molecule, ∑fi = 1.
(5)
I 0
/ I =
∑
i = 1
m
f i
1 +
K
SV , i
∙
C Q
- 1

With Formula (5) on hand, it is rational to assume that there are at least two kinds of Ru(II) probe molecules, one of them is open for oxygen molecule attack and thus highly efficient for oxygen sensing, the other, however, might be covered by surrounding molecules or structural blocks, and is inefficient for oxygen sensing. In this case, a corrected Stern-Volmer equation shall be listed as Formula (6).
(6)
R 0
/ R =
1
f 1
1 +
K
SV , 1
∙
C
O 2
+
f 2
1 +
K
SV , 2
∙
C
O 2

With this corrected Stern-Volmer equation, R0/R against CO2 is fitted and shown in Fig. 9
. Corresponding fitting parameters are given in Table 2. A good fitting is observed, with R2 of 99.9%. Never the less, KSV,2 is observed as an illogical one, −0.008657. A negative KSV value means increasing emission intensity with increasing oxygen concentration, which is not possible for a Ru(II) probe. Considering that this KSV value is quite close to 0, it is assumed that KSV,2 = 0, which means that some Ru(II) probe molecules remain their emission intensity upon various oxygen concentrations. In this case, Stern-Volmer equation is further corrected as Formula (7) [17]. Here f means the ratio emission contribution of probe molecule to total emission at dissolve oxygen concentration of 0. A good fitting is finally observed, with R2 of 99.9%. It is thus confirmed that assuming some Ru(II) probe molecules remain their emission intensity upon various oxygen concentrations is correct. They might be physically covered by chains or groups on CDs surface.
(7)
R 0
R 0
- R
=
1
f ∙
K
SV
∙
C
O 2
+
1 f

Table 2 compares fitting parameters of these Stern-Volmer equations. It is clear that the corrected Stern-Volmer fits experiment data better than the ideal one does within the whole working region of 0.72–42.14 mg/L. As a consequence, the final working equation of Ru@CD for dissolved oxygen is given as Formula (8). Here CO2 means dissolved oxygen concentration, R means detected F627/F432 value.
(8)
C
o 2
=
27.14
R - 0.1976
- 12.97

Selectivity has always been mentioned as an important parameter since analyte molecules are usually dispersed in a complicated environment full of competing species. A precise and reliable result depends on the unique sensing response towards a specific analyte, known as selectivity. To assess the selectivity of Ru@CD, various interferents (100 μmol/L) are added into de-gassed water sample containing Ru@CD (0.1 mg/mL). Since oxygen is a strong oxidizer, two typical oxidative and reductive interferents, H2O2 and glutathione (GSH) are selected. Some common metal cations in natural water, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, are selected. Fig. 10 shows F627/F432 contrast bars of Ru@CD under these interferents. Corresponding emission spectra are given as Figure S2 (Supporting Information). Regardless of their oxidative and reductive nature, H2O2 and GSH have slim effect on Ru@CD, unlike the case in electrochemical analysis. This is because the optical sensing of Ru(II)-based probes is based on a dynamic collision between O2 and Ru(II) probe molecules, not a classical oxidation-reduction reaction. As two widely existed metal cations in nature water, Ca2+ and Mg2+ have neglectable effect on Ru@CD, owing to their weak interaction with both Ru(II) probe and CDs. Transition metal cations, especially Hg2+, have obvious effect on Ru@CD, however. A maximum sensing error of ~8% is observed in the presence of Hg2+. There are NH and NH2 groups on CDs surface. Hg2+ tends to coordinate with these groups, which consequently quenches CDs emission. As a result, F627/F432 shows positive deviation in the presence of Hg2+. In addition, H+ is selected as well since CDs have been reported sensitive towards environment acidity. Similar positive deviation is observed for H+ since it quenches CDs emission as well, leading to positive deviation (see Figure S3, Supporting Information). In pH region of 3–5, such positive deviation is obvious, with F627/F432 value falls in a region of 2.20–2.11, compared to F627/F432 value of 2.02 at pH = 7.0. Never the less, Ru@CD still shows good selectivity over most interferents, with sensing errors no higher than 5%.

To make sure its reliable sensing result, a sensor is supposed to give instant response as soon as analyte concentration is changed, with no hysteresis. Fig. 11 shows F627/F432 values of Ru@CD aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL) when being bubbled by pure N2 and pure O2 streams periodically. It is observed that F627/F432 is effectively quenched to its minimal level under oxygen-deficient condition. Its value is then full recovered to its maximum level under oxygen-rich condition. Such cycle can be fully repeated for at least five times, with no obvious hysteresis. This fact confirms the good signal stability of Ru@CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical sensing in running water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming at a tentative evaluation on the practical sensing performance of Ru@CD, three running water samples are selected, their dissolved oxygen levels are determined with standard analytical method (Winkler) and Ru@CD, respectively. Corresponding result is listed in Table 3 . It is observed that Ru@CD has shown previse result in these samples with sensing errors lower than 4%. It is notable that all three samples have shown positive derivations. This is because there are metal cations in running water, such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+. As above mentioned, their existence tends to quench CDs emission, leading to positive derivation. Another fact to be mentioned is that blank running water shows weak blue fluorescence upon excitation of 350 nm (see Figure S4, Supporting Information), which may be emitted from microbes and/or organic molecules and/or their derivations during water processing. These two factors both exert effect on Ru@CD, resulting in these positive derivations.

For comparison convenience, key sensing parameters of Ru@CD and some literatures are listed in Table 4 . It is observed that literatures of similar self-calibrated sensing systems are still yet to be satisfied. Most of these literatures have their sensing performance only for O2 gas. Some literature sensing systems suffer from non-linear working curves. Some literature sensing systems have shown limited sensitivity for dissolved oxygen. It is clear that Ru@CD is superior to literature systems by showing self-calibrated sensing curve with good linearity. It is clear that Ru@CD is superior to literature systems by showing self-calibrated sensing curve with good linearity and improved sensitivity for dissolved oxygen. In this case, it is safe to say that a promising method for the detection of dissolved O2 in aqueous medium has been established via a covalent hybrid structure of carbon-dots&Ru. Compared to COTS (commercial off-the-shelf) optical emitting oxygen sensing films or optical fibers, which are usually based on emission dynamic quenching technique, Ru@CD provides a candidate pathway of self-calibrated sensing, aiming at improved selectivity and linearity [18].

As a conclusion, we constructed a hybrid structure for the optical sensing of dissolved oxygen in aqueous media, using Ru(II)-bpy as sensing probe and CDs as water-compatible supporting matrix. Ru@CD was identified with AFM, XRD and IR to confirm its structure. Detailed analysis on its emission spectra suggested that it showed self-calibrated sensing behavior towards dissolved oxygen. Its sensing response was analyzed with Stern-Volmer equation (ideal mode and corrected mode). Finally, a corrected Stern-Volmer equation (Lehrer mode) was confirmed. Its working equation was fitted with linearity of 99.1%. Its sensitivity (3.18) was higher than most literature values for dissolved oxygen detection. Good selectivity and signal stability were observed. For further effort, sensitivity should be improved by using Ru(II) probes or other probes with long excited state lifetimes.

Yanping Zhao: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Xuelian Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Jiang: Investigation. Li Mao: Investigation. Hongjun Wang: Investigation, Supervision. : . Liang Liu: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
